speaker: interviewer
text: --How they were spoken. But you said actions were important, please explain this to us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, action meaning if you washed, too often, your hands, you were Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because the Jews are washing their hands more often. If you-- I don't know. My wife would explain it better because she is the Christian. There's a lot of things which you-- you put your towel over your shoulder.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your towel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, over the shoulder, that's Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because we used to do it in Europe like that. And the Jews used to do those things which are very, very thing which are Jewish. You see sometimes--

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: --or you are ask about something which is typical Christian, you haven't got the answer. Which I didn't have, my wife did.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she was made aware before she went on the Aryan side--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, she was learning a lot during--

speaker: interviewer
text: By instinct.

speaker: interviewee
text: By instinct, and by simply learning, reading about the religion, about everything. She used to take girls from another religion, not Catholics, or-- she used to take them to the church and explain them. Because they had been Protestants already, she explained them how to behave there, what to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Excuse me, when did Luba do this?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was a typical Christian.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did Luba do this, that she took other religions to the church to explain how a Catholic lived?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, she used to walk around.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Poland she did this.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And with the--

speaker: interviewee
text: During the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Occupation.

speaker: interviewee
text: During the occupation.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: She behaved better than a Pole than I did, and I had been in Polish school only and speaking only Polish, and she used to speak Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the family that Luba lived with, were they--

speaker: interviewee
text: Religious.

speaker: interviewer
text: --religious Catholics?

speaker: interviewee
text: Super religious.

speaker: interviewer
text: Religious Catholics.

speaker: interviewee
text: No-- oh the ones which she used to live with?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, Catholics.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did she go to church with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: In some cases, in some places, yes. She was very good.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you joined Luba on the Aryan side, did you both live in this family, or did-- with this family?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we lived together, and separate-- that's how the circumstances.

speaker: interviewer
text: And once you moved out of the ghetto, did you-- we're talking the year that you moved out as what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: But I moved out and in again during the resistance.

speaker: interviewer
text: During the resistance. So you moved back in again?

speaker: interviewee
text: In again, because when I shot the policeman I moved back because I was frightened to stay there. Because I knew few people which went walking with me, Christians, who knew my name.

speaker: interviewer
text: So just a second, you talked-- you said you moved back with the resistance when the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Myself, I went out already because we had been disbanded. About the fourth or the third, we'd been disbanded.

speaker: interviewer
text: The fourth or the third, that's the third month, March or April.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, we'd been-- it started in April.

speaker: interviewer
text: April, 1943.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, in April that start in 19th of April-- from the 18th to the 19th of April. Then I went out in May, about the 3rd or something, I went out.

speaker: interviewer
text: To the Aryan side.

speaker: interviewee
text: To the other side. And that's what I say happened on the 3rd, with the policeman.

speaker: interviewer
text: You went out on the Aryan side as David Landau?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, as Jan [NON-ENGLISH], as a Christian.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get false papers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Again.

speaker: interviewer
text: The same person.

speaker: interviewee
text: The same person who give to my wife--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and please tell us--

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: --what happened with the policeman.

speaker: interviewee
text: With the policeman happened that he start to ask me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what policeman please?

speaker: interviewee
text: A Polish policeman, he asked me--

speaker: interviewer
text: On the Aryan side.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh the Aryan side. He asked me questions. And I said, hold on a minute, and drew out a pistol, and shot him. And then the Underground, the Polish Underground, advised in the article when they write about the young Landau, the tailor's son from [NON-ENGLISH] killed him. They are making it known there that the policeman was working with the-- he was a [NON-ENGLISH]-- mean he was working with the Germans. Because on this side, he did have two boys standing with him, how to take over, and to milk me out.

speaker: interviewer
text: To milk you out of money, or of--

speaker: interviewee
text: Of money, of everything he can, or addresses, or things like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: But if--

speaker: interviewee
text: So when I shot them, I started to run, knowing that I got two people there, I-- again, I got the names, and I will keep the names. When I run back, I told myself those people know me. And did advise the Underground already who did it.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Polish Underground or the Jewish Underground?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Polish Underground.

speaker: interviewer
text: That it was Da--

speaker: interviewee
text: Because the Polish Underground is writing this article.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they advised that it was Jan?

speaker: interviewee
text: That it was David Landau from the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: David, what gave you the instinct that this Polish policeman was working with the Germans? Or was it--

speaker: interviewee
text: The way he behaved with two boys on this side, is only two things, to milk me out, which I haven't got anything except a gun, only my document and the gun-- you see, I got three lives with me, no more. I got my own life is my life. My gun is my life. And my document is my life. If he's going to rob one of those three, he's robbing my life. So I'm not going to give it away.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you--

speaker: interviewee
text: And having the boys around, and that he felt to ask me, nobody else, but ask me the document in a street of people, I knew what it meant. And I am guilty. I am a Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: So just a second. In a street full of people he just selected you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Definitely selected me.

speaker: interviewer
text: He just walked straight to you or just happened to pass you and ask you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no no, he passed-- he was coming in from about four or five steps from me. And he-- straight on me. To me that looks, first, like somebody pushed a finger. But I can't say, I can't say. What was the way of having a gun in the ghetto, or outside the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: For you, personally?

speaker: interviewee
text: For me, personally. As an Underground man, as a private person, what was it for me? And what does it mean? And why did I keep it? Death penalty for it, for a gun. But the Germans overdone a little bit. Being a Jew, death penalty. Wearing-- not wearing the armband, the Jewish, death penalty. For having false papers, death penalty. For having a piece of fur on my--

speaker: interviewee
text: Lapel.

speaker: interviewee
text: --Lapel, death penalty. For having false money, which we did have, death penalty.

speaker: interviewer
text: Forged money.

speaker: interviewee
text: Forged money. For having forged papers, Polish papers, death penalty. For being on the Christian side, death penalty. He did give me so many death penalties, that I decided to have the eighth and the ninth and the tenth. That was the gun.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had nothing to lose.

speaker: interviewee
text: He can't kill me eight times.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had nothing to lose.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing to lose. He forced me to be what I have been during the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: So David, what date was this that you killed the police-- what date did you kill the policeman?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the 3rd of May, 1943.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so you felt the need to go back into the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I had been the quickest runner the world did have. I would be the best Olympian till today.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't get--

speaker: interviewee
text: I would get the first medal the--

speaker: interviewer
text: The gold medal.

speaker: interviewee
text: Three gold medals.

speaker: interviewer
text: In one.

speaker: interviewee
text: For running.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, David, so where did you run to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Back to the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you got-- and now, you went in the gate, or through the tunnel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Under the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Underneath, OK. Going back into the ghetto, what did you do? What did you say? Did you tell anybody that you'd shot a--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, yes, oh yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: The shot, yes I told a lot of boys.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then you went back--

speaker: interviewee
text: They'd been surprised that I'm  back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then you-- then what did you do? You acted as an individual? Or you rejoined with ZZW?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I still had been with the boys little bit.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, doing what?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, because the boys have been disbanded.

speaker: interviewer
text: Disbanded.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, now what was there is-- I went to see some friends there. And I said that's a good bunker there.

speaker: interviewer
text: A good bunker.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah, I went downstairs with my bunker-- to one bunker. And on the 12th of May, we'd been taken out of the bunker by the Germans and covered--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this bunker?

speaker: interviewee
text: The bunker was, at that time, at [NON-ENGLISH] factory, [NON-ENGLISH] place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Factory [NON-ENGLISH] place.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did have a brother-in-law, they said come to us, stay with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many were in the bunker?

speaker: interviewer
text: All of you are, sort of, fighting people?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, two fighting people, and the rest no fighting.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Her brother and me.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in the bunker for?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the bunker, I be in there from the 3rd-- from the 3rd, or the 4th, because I'd been with the boys on Muranowska another couple of guys. Not already-- not fighting because-- and we been-- I went to [NON-ENGLISH] then went to the bunker. On the 12th we'd been discovered.

speaker: interviewer
text: By the Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: By the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was the bunker discovered?

speaker: interviewee
text: With dogs or with listening--

speaker: interviewer
text: Hearing, sound, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I opened the top. And I hold the bundle of grenades on a string.

speaker: interviewer
text: On a string?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, and I said, look if you don't come out, not gas, nothing, we drop the grenades. Scream, and cry, and everything, woman want to get out. There were more men than women. I think about six women. And come out, come out, come out, come out. Let's come out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they say come out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they say come out, or did they use another word?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who, the Germans?

speaker: interviewer
text: The Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, come out, come out.

speaker: interviewer
text: In English?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Not in English, no. In German.

speaker: interviewer
text: What word did they use?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know German because I didn't know Jewish well. So they told us to [NON-ENGLISH]. We went outside. And I put us on the ground, and the face down I'd been looking a little bit on the side. With us, had been two Germans, which is weird to say about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Two Germans in the bunker?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two Germans in the bunker, Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, OK, fine. Yes, go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish Germans, 48, 49.

speaker: interviewer
text: Their age?

speaker: interviewee
text: Age.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long had they been in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe even older. They had been in the bunker together with the people, I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, had they come from German--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they had been thrown out of Germany to Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, fine.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the parents, or somebody-- with two boxes, cigar boxes, both of them. We thought first when we'd seen it that they are holding the boxes, that those are probably money there. But we are not interested in it. Down in the bunkers we're waiting for our death--

speaker: interviewer
text: So, all right, so--

speaker: interviewee
text: --for our penalty.

speaker: interviewer
text: Excuse me, when you lay on your faces on the ground, you were already outside the bunker, correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: We been outside the bunker. And they came out with the two boxes. And when they said to them to lie down, they didn't. But they salute to the young officer about 22, 23. The SS man rolled up his sleeves. I could see the whole lot with my eye lying down. He was rolled up--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who rolled up their sleeve?

speaker: interviewee
text: The German officer, the SS man, the Gestapo was rolling-- did have rolled up there, standing in front of them. And they said in German, which Czechoslovak boy, later, which was with us-- again, thrown out or running away from Czechoslovakia told me exactly what he said.

speaker: interviewer
text: Translated.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I didn't know German.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and what it--

speaker: interviewee
text: They asked-- they said to the German that I came in 1920 to Europe from the States to fight for the German Army. And open the boxes, and the crosses are in there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, medals.

speaker: interviewee
text: Medals, we're told that's money. That medals which I hold--

speaker: interviewer
text: In the cigar boxes, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: The very moment he said it, he took out the revolver--

speaker: interviewer
text: The German officer.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, and shot both between the eyes, both of them. He didn't wait a minute. And I seen the things falling down but not knowing what it is. That was-- we thought that's after them, we are going. But no, they sent us to Umschlag, where I went to the Umschlag. I went to train--

speaker: interviewer
text: The assembly place.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we jumped from the train on the way to Treblinka.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know where the train was taking you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we knew. We had been told about it, that it was Treblinka. Because at that time, they took to two places.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the Treblinka track.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many people were on the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: How many?

speaker: interviewer
text: People on the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say-- on the train, a few thousand. But I would say that was about-- in our place, in our--

speaker: interviewer
text: Carriage.

speaker: interviewee
text: --it was about 100, 105. We had been sitting one of the top of another one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right, the door was sealed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sealed.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you left the station?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And yet you said you jumped out.

speaker: interviewee
text: About 20 kilometers before Treblinka. Because remember one thing, 16 kilometers before Treblinka, you can't jump.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please explain.

speaker: interviewee
text: You can't jump because from there, are the Germans taking over the train.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the Poles take the train to six-- took the train--

speaker: interviewee
text: 16 Kilometers before Treblinka. So if someone will tell you, that you jumped out at the 16, is one in a trillion.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did you were approximately, or you were 20 kilometers from Treblinka?

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew what we were passing. We knew places. We knew what we'd had been--

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you were in sealed carriages. How can you jump out?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, up in the windows on-- you looked through the windows.

speaker: interviewer
text: You look through the window.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah with the bars. You can see out.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you can release the bars?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we cut them.

speaker: interviewer
text: With what?

speaker: interviewee
text: With saws. Everyone did have--

speaker: interviewer
text: With soles?

speaker: interviewee
text: With their saw.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh saw.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We did have, every one of us, every one which was in the Underground or young did have a saw with them all the time, in the bunkers, outside the bunkers, in the shoe, or in the stocking we did have a saw.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when they loaded you onto the train, the didn't check--

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: --anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not when they took you out from this.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were put on the train, were you given any food, or facilities, or did you have any toilet facilities?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nothing, no food.

speaker: interviewee
text: Dead.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right, so you jump from the train at 20 kilometers. You on your own?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, three of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Three of you. Were you all from the underground? Still today, Nicholas can you get that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, yes, here and here. Here and here. That's from the-- you know, from the stones.

speaker: interviewer
text: The gravel.

speaker: interviewee
text: Gravel, which is there. Yeah, I got it still. Oh, yes and how.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the three of you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Three of us jumped.

speaker: interviewer
text: --were from the Underground?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon? No, not from the Underground at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just you.

speaker: interviewee
text: The other two hadn't been from the Underground.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was it three men, three women?

speaker: interviewee
text: Three men.

speaker: interviewer
text: Three men.

speaker: interviewee
text: And to come to the window, I was lucky. I was the one staying near the window because I thought of jumping. Those two boys had been pushed in first. And I'd been there in the back. To come to me was a fight, inside, a tremendous fight. Because as soon as they lie on their heads, the ones that which did have enough strength pushed them back because everybody wants to be near the window. Because you can see-- you've got a chance. You've got-- you see something. That's very important, very important.

speaker: interviewer
text: So David, from these two joined you at the window area and you jumped out. And what happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well first of all, we threw out my jacket to see if someone of the guards is not standing--

speaker: interviewer
text: Will notice it.

speaker: interviewee
text: No shot, nothing happened. So the first one jumped down. The jumping is this way. You're taking those bars in because it's easier when you've got them in, to push him out. They're not very big one, that finger is-- hard enough. We were cutting it with the music of the train.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, with a sound disguise.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sound. [MIMICKING SAW NOISE] And that's how we were cutting it. Stop, we don't touch it, back. You don't taking of any one of those to pull them till the three are cut, or four are cut-- four, I think we did it. When they are cut, we're pushing them up inside. Because inside, three of us can push it. Outside, we can't because of that's very hard. So we pushed them out-- we pushed them in. We-- going first out, we-- you've got to lift people. I'm in very big trouble. You push them out with their legs out, on the tummy. With the front out, and you grab the thing, and then you pull yourself out from the train. Then he pushed himself with his legs to fall as far as he can. I did have a problem.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I was the last one. And the boys didn't want to help too much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because we pushed ourselves in, and-- not too much to me, but another one. They didn't want to help too much. But two boys said all right, we'll give you a hand. And I-- standing about three rows away, they pushed themselves in, young people. And they helped me to get out. I got a very bad fall. I've been told to move my legs when you run from a train. I've been told before, much before, how to run in case of a train. I didn't move my legs too quick. And-- [CLAPPING HANDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I got a fall. And I-- Did the damage.

speaker: interviewee
text: The third boy, which knew a little bit about it, which was very clever. And he said David-- he ripped off my shirt, bottom of the shirt. He said urine on it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, urine.

speaker: interviewee
text: Urine on it, yes. And he started to put them around here, make them a knot. And that's what I held.

speaker: interviewer
text: Acted as an antiseptic.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I held it. But I couldn't walk it. And we walked three days to Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the three of you got out, and you walked back three days to Warsaw.

speaker: interviewee
text: To Warsaw, yes, because we only walked in the nighttime.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, did you-- were you able to acquire any food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, tree-- bark of the tree and rotten potatoes or something like that, and flowers.

speaker: interviewer
text: And flowers.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And water?

speaker: interviewee
text: We ate flowers. We did have enough water, yes, plenty. Because whatever we passed there was a--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you pass any people along the way?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we avoided them.

speaker: interviewer
text: You avoided them.

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew about them but--

speaker: interviewer
text: When you arrived back in Warsaw, what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Caught again. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: By whom?

speaker: interviewee
text: By the Germans and the Poles.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: They took us to a little camp which was a camp where they coll--

speaker: interviewer
text: Collected.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Collected.

speaker: interviewee
text: Collected the Jews in the camp. And then when they got about 300-400, they sent them, again, to the gas chamber-- to the gas--

speaker: interviewer
text: You knew it was gas chambers?

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew. We knew--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right, so you were-- where did they hold you for that?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Warsaw. In Warsaw they hold us. You see--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it, do you know exactly where it was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, [NON-ENGLISH] three.

speaker: interviewer
text: [NON-ENGLISH] three.

speaker: interviewee
text: Where workrooms, all the workrooms of the [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: All, right workshops.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Now I want to tell you something about happening, who was caught and how caught. That wasn't a rule or regulation that I kept caught three, and I wouldn't shoot them on the spot, or take him to shoot, or to take him to prison, or to send him immediately. It wasn't a rule or regulation. Whoever wants, did whatever he did want. Because why didn't they shoot me on the spot there? Or take me to a place like [NON-ENGLISH] 5, where they killed the people on the spot?

speaker: interviewer
text: Because they sometimes took out of the bunkers, and they'll shoot people. They found them in the street, they shot them. One, why play around? No, they decided-- the gendarme, the police, [NON-ENGLISH] decided to take us to this camp [NON-ENGLISH]. You know [NON-ENGLISH], with the [INAUDIBLE]. He decided that he will take us to the camp or [NON-ENGLISH]. Order, has to be.

speaker: interviewee
text: Order has to be. So he took us to the camp. He left us in the camp. I ran from the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Excuse me?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a full story about running.

speaker: interviewer
text: You ran from the camp? You weren't in a locked room?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, no in the locked camp, in the camp, with a yard, with everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how--

speaker: interviewee
text: With barbed wire only about four or five foot high, no more. But with a very big guard around-- with a big guard--

speaker: interviewer
text: A guard of what?

speaker: interviewee
text: A guard of the helpers of the German--

speaker: interviewer
text: Of soldiers.

speaker: interviewee
text: Soldiers, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Armed guards around the whole area.

speaker: interviewee
text: A tremendous guard, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did you run from there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two boys helped me, two Ukrainians.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ukrainian Jews, or Ukrainian--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, Ukrainian--

speaker: interviewer
text: Soldiers.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they said that I'm a Christian.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, that you said you were a Christian, or they said?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they said to me, what are you doing here? I said I don't know. He said, you are Pole. I said definitely, I am a Pole. He said, so how come? I said, I cleaned up some Jews, and they took me with them. So they said, all right, in the nighttime will help you to get out. I thought that they were not going to help me. They are going to shoot me when I go out, and show it to the men which was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: The body.

speaker: interviewee
text: The German in civilian cloth was watching the place. In charge of it was a gentleman in civilian cloth.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Only with the hat-- in the hat they did have the [NON-ENGLISH]. Why? Don't ask, I don't know. I didn't ask them. But what is there-- what was there-- they said that I'm a Pole. So I thought to myself, if they let me out, or whatever, they let me out, they'll shoot me and say to the German, look how good we're watching it. But I'm not risking anything. Because to be free, for a Jew from 1939 till 1945, for me, was to be free mean dead.

speaker: interviewer
text: So if you're going to tell me, during that time, David, you free, I won't believe you. You got to have good proof to prove to me that I am free. That means I got to see the first 50 soldiers, Americans, with the flag. I have to wake up myself, if that's true, that I see them, have to touch them. To me, was only one thing, to be sure that I am free mean dead.

speaker: interviewee
text: Only a dead Jew is free, free from the torture, free from the thinking, free from the running. Otherwise, does not exist free. Because I've been asked by a Jewish fella at the end of the war to get out of a bunker. And I didn't want to get out because I thought that Germans are standing in the back of him and shooting-- want him to shoot.

speaker: interviewer
text: David, let's get back to you to this. Two Ukrainians helped you out of the camp that was surrounded by guards even though the barbed wire was four to five feet high.

speaker: interviewee
text: No they did give me a shovel. A sugar shovel.

speaker: interviewer
text: A sugar shovel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that's a little shovel. And they said to stay in toilet, which was very close to the barbed wire. They said in the nighttime, about 12 o'clock, we'll go with the brother up in the outside. And we will whistle a song, a Russian song. And I will never forget the Russian song, my life. [SPEAKING RUSSIAN]

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you translate that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you translate that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I can't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you whistle that tune?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I can't whistle it at all, but I can-- [SPEAKING UKRAINIAN] Maybe the gentleman will know what it is. But that's Ukrainian. And he said, when I will sing that song, or whistle that song, you go out. But 12 o'clock, start to dig the little hole underneath. They did give me a hat from a train conductor, there, or a worker. A special train hat-- driver hat. He did give me that. And he said when we whistle it, go out and run. Because that wasn't a controlled there with counting for this--

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long did it take you to dig? Were you ready for when they whistled--

speaker: interviewee
text: The digging?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: The digging-- at 12 o'clock I went out of the toilet because I'd been hiding there. It was very close to-- you see, toilets and things like that, the Germans didn't want to go there near it. Because I'd been worried.

speaker: interviewer
text: But David you--

speaker: interviewee
text: I went out of there. It took me about no more than 10 minutes. Because it wasn't hard. That was only to dig out enough not to slide underneath.

speaker: interviewer
text: To crawl under.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where-- how long between from your digging till they sing the song, approximately?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that was about 10 minutes, 15 minutes, I came along--

speaker: interviewer
text: So on the outside, what happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went out, knowing that they're going to shoot, knowing. But I learned something during the resistance, that's when you run away from a thing like that. You're running in a zigzag so it's harder-- I told her to switch off the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Go on.

speaker: interviewee
text: I told her. She didn't took off the telephone.

speaker: interviewer
text: David--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you ran in a zigzag fashion.

speaker: interviewee
text: I ran in a zigzag, and ran away from there. I did run away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they shoot after you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not at all. And I went-- and it was very early in the night, so I got to hide there [INAUDIBLE] again.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was surrounding you that you were able to hide?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the surrounding was a field with the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Haystack?

speaker: interviewee
text: A field, a complete field, about two, three kilometers field. So with the railway-- what do you call it?

speaker: interviewer
text: Culvert.

speaker: interviewee
text: Culvert, yes. So I run. I hid there in the old house, in the burned down house. I hide till about 8 o'clock in the morning with the hat. I was very, very dark, completely black from the sun. And I went into a tram, without a penny.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tram or train?

speaker: interviewee
text: Tram to my wife. But in the tram, I played-- I start to play that I am drunk. And I start to talk about the Germans and what they did to us. So everyone from the front--

speaker: interviewer
text: From that part of the tram.

speaker: interviewee
text: --part of the tram, start to move out. That was not the tram-- it was a tram, but that was the first-- before you come in to the covered tram. It's only the--

speaker: interviewer
text: The open section.

speaker: interviewee
text: --the open section. And start to talk about the Germans bad. And everyone, on the first stop, walked out. They thought, either I am a drunk which doesn't know what he's talking about, or I'm a provocateur talking about the Germans. You want to know what the people say. So they moved out. But unfortunately, next stop, another idiot came in. So I got to play around again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you--

speaker: interviewee
text: And I played till I came to the state I got to go up. I had been a drunkard, what can I do? [MUMBLING] [HICCUP] [MUMBLING] [HICCUP] That Hitler is a swine, and is very, very bad to us. And the Germans are killing us. And everybody wake up to do something about it. [HICCUP] And everybody walked out of the tram. Then I went further a little bit. Again, people came up, and then again, down. Till I went to my wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Luba had known you were taken on the train originally.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, she didn't know anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: She didn't know anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you came-- when you reunited with her--

speaker: interviewee
text: The people didn't want to take me in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: They took me in eventually because--

speaker: interviewer
text: Which people?

speaker: interviewee
text: The people which Luba was there with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, why didn't the want to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they said that I am burned from the ghetto, and someone will see me will recognize.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, we convinced her, and she let us stay a few days. And we moved to another place.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have anywhere to move, or do you find--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we prepared. We did have a lot of Christian--

speaker: interviewer
text: Contacts.

speaker: interviewee
text: Contacts, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so what happened then, David?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we'd been during the time of the Christian side, we'd been going from one place to another one. We did that for a few bad things too. I went, once, to a place, for example, where I went to a party of 20 people which was [NON-ENGLISH]. That's when the birthday [NON-ENGLISH] of the holy [NON-ENGLISH]. And the [NON-ENGLISH] sits at his place, the [NON-ENGLISH], which is now called [NON-ENGLISH], got the birthday.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's a saint, is it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, but the-- if you-- Oh Jesus Christ.

speaker: interviewer
text: Come on, David, as the saint and-- yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the men, which got a name Janek, got a birthday, like, a party. Now when I came in home-- it's a very important thing-- when I came in to that girl, she opened the door, and she said Janek, how are you? I shouldn't be there. I should be sitting in my home and waiting for my guests. That's something very unusual. And 20 people looking at my face, and that was no good. I immediately felt very, very bad. That they may smell something.

speaker: interviewer
text: They featured you.

speaker: interviewee
text: That I am Jan, and I am coming there. So she was very smart, again. She's seen what she did. And she bent down to one of the boys and start-- told them something. And the word came across, around in one second, like a flash. And they all got up with glasses of vodka, took up and started to sing 100 years, 100 years, to live as long, Janek. I start to cry because-- [CRYING] sorry for this.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's fine.

speaker: interviewee
text: They sang the song. She said to them after when it's happened, and I went, she told me. She said to him that I am a boy from the forest, that I'm an Underground boy, a Christian.

speaker: interviewer
text: Partisan.

speaker: interviewee
text: But partisan. And for them a Janek Partisan got nowhere to go anyhow. So he's coming anywhere he wants to, and he got a friend. That doesn't mean for me any party anything because he got to find a place because he got his home is the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Is the forest.

speaker: interviewee
text: --the forest. So they sang, and they start to talk to me friendly and nice. And that was the end of it when I get out of the problem. But I thought that I'm--
